speaker: interviewer
text: Betty Cohen. Betty, more about liberation and what happened after.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The people that we stayed with were just elated, of course, that we had made it. And we were elated that, you know, that nothing had happened to them. So our main goal after the war was to make sure that these people would be remunerated in one way or the other. Their house, half of their house, had been shut down, the bottom-- the back part. So we wanted to make sure that they would get everything that they needed. Because the farmer was a poor farmer. There was no question about it. Never asked for a penny, offered us everything that we wanted. One of the incidents that I remember, the little nephew who lived with them for the last year maybe, or maybe even longer than that, he got a pair of sandals. And, I mean, here I was. And I looked at the new sandals and I thought they were beautiful. And I hadn't been out for two years. So-- [GASPS] Oh, beautiful. The sandals.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first thing that Agnes asked was, would you like a pair? I just take a few eggs and go over there, and I'll, I'll just trade them. I'll just barter. And of course, of course, we didn't do that. But I'm just saying that, this is the attitude of these people. You had them made into righteous Christians in the Yad Vashem, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they come to Israel for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't want to go to Israel. But the second couple, the Ali and Tima couple, we were able to send them to Israel, because my older brother, Jos, has been in Israel ever since-- through World War II. I don't not know-- oh, the same-- as long as we have been in the United States. My brother lives in [INAUDIBLE], has been there since 1956. From the Dutch army, he went into the Israeli army, and went from one rank to the other. I cannot tell them all to you because I don't remember anymore. Lieutenant, colonel, I think he was in the Israeli army. And he was able to give these people a house to stay in and show them Israel. And give them-- And have been honored by Yad Vashem in person. I was absolutely delighted. It's the only thing that we wanted after the war to happen. We didn't ask anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, Betty. Let me ask then, who survived and who did not survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of our household, my parents died in concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz. I do not have the dates. It took us a long, long time before we found the dates. My uncle's family, who absolutely-- who lived next door-- I think I gave you the name originally. My uncle, Leopold. He had five kids. Only one, the only daughter they had, survived. Four people passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: All died in concentration camp. Uncle Sally, the whole family died in concentration camp. Only my father's younger brother, Maurice, survived. And he was actually the one who gave us-- right after the war, after we came back from the hiding-- he gave us 100 guilders, which was a lot of money for us because we didn't have a penny. We absolutely had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we did not go back to our house because I think two families lived in the same house. I wouldn't go back to the street even. Anything at all that was related to my parents, I did not want to see. I remember two things. We had-- I got one set of underwear from the Red Cross. That's what I had. Then, I did not have any clothing, or hardly any clothing, but I did have covers of-- bed covers that were on the down blankets. And since I knew how to sew, I took the covers from the blankets and made a dress for my sister and for myself. Then, I did the same thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had never seen a movie, what is called-- oh, yeah. Doesn't matter. It's a movie where the girl takes the curtains from the windows, or some-- what is it? whatever--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's not it plays also in Austria. I've forgotten now what the title is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't matter. The girl took the curtains from the windows and made it into dresses, which is exactly what I did. The only thing that I had to wear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know-- you say that your parents perished in camp. Did you know that there were such things as concentration camps while you were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my mother's brother in 19-- I think it was 1935, '36, he lived in Germany. And he already went to a concentration camp. At that time, however, since he had relatives outside of Germany, we were able to get him out of the camp. So of course, we knew about concentration camps. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about your parents' death?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother in Holland never got over World War II. He lives it daily, 50 years afterwards. He is the one-- he has everything-- oh, he, he had a diary. He has a diary of all the stories, part of it what I told you now. But he has a diary. And he-- as I say-- and I'm not overdoing this at all. I am not saying something that is not true. Right now, he still lives the World War II. He, he found out all things. He now has proof even. He just told me that in a letter, and the letter is here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me that now he has proof that our neighbors, where we used to live, went to Germans and said, here are Jews. And why don't you pick them up? That's how come my parents were picked up. See? So that's-- you know, I know that he has-- my brother has all these things. You told me how your brother's reacted. Now, tell me how you've taken this whole story. How are you different because of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told you already that food was something that I didn't even want to look at. I was-- I didn't know how to express myself in any other way. I think that that's what it was. And I just ate enough to survive. I remember also that Agnes once-- also, she knew, of course, that Jews don't eat pork. She cooked pork once. She did everything for us what was possible. But OK, she cooked pork for us once. And I did not know. And she said, come on. Eat a little bit. Eat a little bit. And I did eat something, not knowing that it was pork. But when I found out maybe half an hour later, all I did, of course, was throw up the whole thing. I just didn't want-- I didn't know how to express myself. Here I was, the youngest. My brother, Harry, and my sister had never-- were never as close as I was with my brother, Harry. We were close in age. And my sister was so much older. And Harry was the sportsman and the soccer player, et cetera. And the runner. And his sister, Betty, who was only two years younger than he was, could do all the things that he could do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was so close to him that he always-- my sister and my brother, I wouldn't say they didn't get along. That is overdoing it. But they were not as close as I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Consequently, especially the last year while we were in Azewijn in this barn where we run hiding, I was the boss. I was absolutely the boss. Also, when the American pilots came, I was the boss. I divided the food. I divided everything. Everybody-- OK, Harry. That's for you. Harry would get two third and, and my sister would get one third, let's say. And I wouldn't eat anything if I possibly could, that kind of thing. And I did that with everything-- with anything that you could think of.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how is that still alive in you today? The anxiety--

speaker: interviewee
text: If I go-- the same thing. I, I think it's the same thing. When I go-- when you-- [LAUGHS] If you would take me to a place, like a buffet, if I see a lot of food, I don't need any food. I just stay away from it. I can't stand it. I didn't know that it was part of my after-war circumstance, but I have always accepted it was a part of me. But I have never even dreamed that that it's because of the war. But I knew that that was-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --Conscientiously. Other than-- other than the food problem, is there something else that changes your life? That changed your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: What should I-- well, of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: How are you different because of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- well, I don't think that I changed that much. Oh, I am less religious. I am less religious. I used to go and go to the temple on a weekly basis, as I told you. We used to live, what I call, the poor section of Beverly Hills after I was married, when we came here. And we used to go to Temple [INAUDIBLE] on [INAUDIBLE]. And I mean, we always went there. And it was a pleasure. Loved it. Loved it. Loved it. But now, I don't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe I should-- it's not nice of me to say this, but I'm going to tell you what I think that it is. I have never even brought it out in words. My parents were the best people in the world, religious and everything. They didn't survive. All right? Nobody helped them. Nobody helped them.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the reasons that I did not go back to Holland just now, 50 years after the war, the Second World War was over, because they honored my parents. And everybody wanted me to come. That was just a couple of months ago in May. Everybody came, but I did not go. I told everybody, I'm sorry, people. You are 50 years late. So I am not going to come. And I think, as I said, I have never expressed it in words, but I think that's what it is. That's really what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're angry at God?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not angry at God. I thank God. I thank God 24 times the other day for something that happened. I'm not angry at God. But I see things in a different manner, I would say, than what I saw then. I was used to certain things, and the-- and, and, the things that-- and maybe there is another thing. And that is not very nice of me to say, but I'm going to say it anyway. I am used, as I said in the beginning, of-- my religion is something that I do at home. I find too much of it here that is ostentatious or brought out on the street. If you don't light the candles on Friday night, you are not a Jew. Well, I'm sorry, people. You don't have to light the candles in order to be a very good Jew. That's what I would like to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't speak about you coming to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to say that. When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to the United States in 1956. October, I think it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you wearing on that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was wearing a suit. In fact, I was wearing the suit when I got on board. I think the ship was a Dutch ship, [NON-ENGLISH], leaving from Rotterdam. And we went on the [NON-ENGLISH] from Rotterdam to New York. Because our sponsors, my friend, Ruth, who I referred to in the beginning of the story, lived in New York. She still lives there. And I was wearing the suit. And since I have no problem in retaining weight and not gaining any weight-- and besides, I'm so disciplined and I'm a yogi to begin with-- I-- today when you said that you were coming today, I thought, well, it would be a very nice way of putting on the same suit that I wore when I came to the United States. And so from 1956 to 1995, I really haven't gained very much And here is my suit if you are interested. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's lovely. It's a lovely one, I should say. You married here, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I married in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: You married in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, my, my husband story's a lovely story. Tiny, little story. My husband was on his bicycle from Hilversum in Holland. And he also had an-- what they called an [NON-ENGLISH]. The Germans had given him an [NON-ENGLISH] so that he could work in the factory that he was working. And the Germans came and they picked up everybody in a big stadium. And everybody was put on transport as slave laborer to Germany. They came halfway through Holland in a little town. I think it was Barneveld. I won't spell it for you. It takes too much-- long.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody got off the bike. And the German soldiers got off the bike because there were-- there were two signs and they didn't know should they go left turn or should they make a right turn in order to get to Germany the quickest way possible?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband got off his bike because there was a little bakery. He goes into the bakery. In front of the bakery are a few women standing there, looking at all this going on. My husband comes out of the bakery, hides between the women in front of the bakery. And the whole troops of Germans and/or Dutch workers went on to Germany. And my husband stood amongst the women and never got to Germany. Of course, it was his saving. And then he went to a friend in another part of Germany-- another part of Holland not too far away from where we were. And he worked there as a laborer on a farm. And so he was not really in hiding like we were. He did not need to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a remarkable man, wasn't he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very remarkable man.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 46 years, and my husband passed away about three months ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: You showed me all your artwork in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all wonderful, including the piece behind you. And he's painted it all, and, and worked in many different mediums, isn't that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- [LAUGHS] In Holland, he was a hat-maker. A hat-maker, felt hats. So when we made plans to come to the United States, he decided, well, he better do something else. So he became a welder. He studied to be a welder, went to school, did everything else, because he was nothing else but aircraft, et cetera. Not-- not-- not knowing that as a non-citizen, you cannot work in an aircraft factory. When we came here, he could not be a welder. Fortunately enough for us, we had friend who-- friends who were electricians. So he learned to be an electrician. And he worked all his life here in the United States as an electrician. And he was a very good one. But one of his best things, he was very inventive. If he had had rich parents-- which he did not-- if he had had rich parents, he would be a top engineer. He was very inventive. He could think things before he would do them or make them. And anything that you see in this house, from lamps to paintings, or whatever you see, is made by him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, what did you do in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first year-- since I knew how to sew, the very first day, I went to downtown Los Angeles. But of course, I couldn't sew the way that these people did with sewing machines. [IMITATES MACHINE] You know, that kind of thing. It didn't work for me. So the lady-- there was a very nice lady who knew somebody who also had come from Holland. She said, you should go to Beverly Hills. There are plenty of places where they make dresses, custom made, like your mother was used to. Go there. I did. I went to Beverly Hills. And within a day, I found a job. I visited lady on Beverly Drive. And her name is Zeda, of all things. I found out later on that is a German name for grandmother or grandfather, one of the two. And I don't speak Yiddish, so that's my shortcoming. I worked with her for about a year and a half. And there was a lady there also who was from Romania. And these two people taught me everything. And this lady now, I am sure she's in her 90's. And I see her still. She used to come here. We would put on concerts for her because she loved the music. And we would put on concerts.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. Then I found out-- I had somebody who I knew. And she said, why don't you learn to type? You can go and work at a bank or something like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to-- oh, we both-- my husband and I, we both graduated Los Angeles High School. Cap and gown and the whole bit and everything from scratch. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had been going to school from the first time that we came here, and for years and years, I went to school. I found a job at the Bank of America, and I stayed there 19 years, from an NCR [? proof ?] operator to-- in a very small branch-- a operations officer. And it was-- had very, very, very good times there also. Very, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're working still?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm still working. Then, after the 19 years of Bank of America, a few things happened that I didn't care for too much. I then went to work for a company, D and E Corporation, it was called at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was the best customer the bank ever had. They knew me. I had done everything for them all the years that I was at the bank. And they asked me, what was I doing? I said, I'm not doing anything. I'm making a deposit here today in the bank. Well, when I came home, there was a phone call. Why don't you come and work for us? And that was 18 years ago, and I'm still there. Wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the company now is called Paragon Homes. They are developers and builders. They are unbelievable people. I'll give you tiny, little example. After I'd been there 10 years, they sent myself and-- they sent me and my husband for 10 days in the most expensive hotel to Hawaii. So I would say they are good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they are good people. Beautiful. I-- I have no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you draw good people to you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: There must be a very good reason for that. Thank you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have some wonderful pictures which I'd like very much to have you show.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to thank you so much for your interview. It's been fascinating.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're a lovely lady to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is a picture of my parents, Benjamin Straus. And my mother, Hedwig Levita Straus. And the picture was taken in Holland, somewhere around 1910. This is a picture of my brothers and sister and my cousins in front of a kindergarten. The wall around the kindergarten in the little town of S'Heenrenberg, Holland, where I was born. I am the number-- in the front line, the front row, number two on the right-hand side. I was fat, as you can see. My sister is right in back of me. My younger brother, Harry, is the one with the sailor suit on. And my older brother, Jos, very-- the very last one on the left-hand side. This is a picture taken in Essen, Germany, about 1925, I would say. On the left-hand side is-- it's me. My name was Betty Straus at the time. Next to me is my aunt, Betty. And on the right-hand side is my brother, Harry. And we are walking, going for lunch in a restaurant to learn good manners, et cetera, et cetera. That's a cute picture. That one is the one-- This is a picture made in-- I think in the beginning of 1942, just before we couldn't do anything anymore. We were able to ride our bicycles, but we were not allowed to be any place otherwise.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our friends, Ali and Tima Beuving, came to have a Sunday afternoon with us. And we went to a little restaurant in our neighborhood. And I'll tell you who I am. I am the one look at you, the closest to you. My name is Betty Cohen Straus.

speaker: interviewee
text: The very right person-- the person on the very right is my sister, Benadine. Next to her is Ali. At that time, Ali [NON-ENGLISH]. And next to her is Tima Beuving. And they are the couple who transported us twice, and who did all the work for everybody. On the left-hand side, very far left, is my brother, Harry. And the other two people were people who worked with us in the same store where I worked in 1938, '39. This picture was taken after the Second World War. It is in Holland. And the couple, the lady on the left and the gentleman on the right, is the farmers couple who were hiding us all this time in the little town of Azewijn. In the middle is my brother, Harry, with his youngest daughter, Alexandra. And of course, the dog is terribly important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names of the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the names of these people. Agnes Garben. My brother is Harry Straus. And Joep Garben is the gentleman on the right. And Alexandra Straus is the little girl in the-- This is a picture of the barn that we were hiding in in Azewijn, Holland, a very small town close to the German border. This was after the war. When we were there, the roof was not as dilapidated as it is on this particular picture. It was-- mm-- the hole was not that big.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the attic was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the attic was our hiding place where we-- where we-- and with us, three Americans, and many other people stayed and were in hiding. And the people who took care of us there was the family Garben, whose picture you just saw two seconds ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the cows lived with you as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the cows lived in the back of this barn. And the front part of the house was not in use. That was only used for us and our hiding place. This is a picture of my husband, Rudy Cohen, and myself, Betty Cohen, going aboard the [NON-ENGLISH], 1956 in Rotterdam, Holland, in order to go to the United States. The suit that I'm wearing there is exactly the same suit that I'm wearing today, September 27, 1995. This is a picture of me, my-- Betty Cohen, just after we came to the United States. I think it was in the following year, 1957, walking on Hauser Avenue. I was already dressed the American way. I had on a blouse that I had bought at Hagerty's, although I had no money. But I bought the blouse anyway. I was used to good clothing. And please notice my white gloves, because that was part of life at the time. This is a picture taken in [NON-ENGLISH], Holland, in 1945. This was the house that my older brother's wife lived while he was in the service of the Dutch-- when my brother was in the service of the Dutch army. Later on, my husband and I lived there, the first years that we were married, in the same house. On the picture, my sister, Dine, wears a sweater that I knitted for her. Next to her, an English soldier who came to liberate us. And we became friendly with him and we were friends for years. Next to it is Betty Cohen. Well, I don't remember the name of the English soldier anymore. Next to it is-- it's Betty Cohen with a dress on that she made herself. And she was very, very proud of that. And next to that is my brother, Harry. And we-- so that's-- so that's I think all that's on the picture. This is a picture of Rudy Cohen taken in September of 1994 and Betty Cohen walking in Santa Monica. We were ardent walkers. And we had company. My niece was here from Holland. And we had company. And they were the ones who gave us-- who sent us the picture later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The end of September, 1994, in Santa Monica. This picture is taken about two years ago, I would say. Maybe three years ago. I'm an ardent yogi, so I stand on my head a lot. This picture was taken in Hawaii, when the company that I worked for sent my husband and myself, Rudy Cohen and Betty Cohen, to Hawaii to celebrate my anniversary with the company.
